description: Heartfelt country song with acoustic warmth, narrative lyrics, and honest vocal delivery
suno_tags: modern country, acoustic guitar, storytelling, male vocals, steel guitar, heartfelt, Americana, 108 BPM
vocal_style: Warm, honest, and conversational with a natural twang. Like telling a story on a porch swing — unhurried and genuine. Slight gravel in the voice adds character. Builds warmth and intensity into the chorus without losing the intimate feel.

tips: Paint pictures with specific details — makes, models, road names, seasons
Write like you're telling a true story over a beer
Chorus should capture the universal feeling behind the specific story
Use the landscape as emotional mirror — weather reflects mood
Bridge reveals the deeper meaning or the lesson learned

example_lyrics:
[Intro]
[Acoustic Guitar]
[Instrumental]

[Verse 1]
Daddy's truck still sits out back
Rusted fender, tires flat
He taught me how to drive on Miller Road
Left hand on the wheel, right hand on the dial
Found a station, made him smile
Said "Son, that's Merle, now that's pure gold"

[Chorus]
Some things rust but they never fade
Like the lessons a good man made
In a two-tone truck on a dirt road ride
I carry him with me, mile by mile

[Verse 2]
Mama kept his flannel shirt
Hung it where his jacket worked
Said the house still smells like him in May
I drive past the old ball field
Where he taught me how to deal
With striking out and getting back to play

[Chorus]
Some things rust but they never fade
Like the lessons a good man made
In a two-tone truck on a dirt road ride
I carry him with me, mile by mile

[Bridge]
Now I've got a kid of my own
Same stubborn chin, same grin he's shown
And when we drive those backroads slow
I hear my daddy's voice, and he knows

[Chorus]
[Powerful]
Some things rust but they never fade
Like the lessons a good man made
In a two-tone truck on a dirt road ride
I carry him with me, mile by mile

[Outro]
Mile by mile
[Acoustic Guitar]
[End]
do: Root the story in specific, tangible details — not abstract feelings
Use acoustic guitar and steel guitar as signature sounds
Let the chorus be the emotional takeaway — what the story means
Write in a conversational, natural speaking rhythm
Connect generations — family stories resonate deeply in country
dont: Don't use every country cliche at once — pick specific details over tropes
Don't lose the story in the chorus — keep the narrative thread
Don't over-produce — acoustic authenticity is the genre's strength
Don't write in overly poetic language — country is plain-spoken
Don't forget the emotional arc — verses set up, chorus pays off
